title: Test Data Management in CI: What Actually Works (and What Looks Good in Talks But Fails in Practice)
excerpt: Most test data strategies sound reasonable until you run 40 parallel jobs in GitHub Actions and watch them all fight over the same database rows. Here's what I've learned from cleaning up those messes.
content:
*By Abdulkadir Akyurt*

There's a specific kind of pain that comes from watching your CI pipeline turn into a flaky nightmare at 2am before a release. Half your tests are failing with constraint violations, deadlocks, or stale data left behind by the last run. Someone committed a fix, you re-ran the suite, and now a different half is failing. The data is in an unknown state and nobody knows whose fault it is.

I've been there more than once. And the root cause, almost every time, is that test data management was treated as an afterthought — something you figure out after you've written the tests. The tests themselves get careful attention: good selectors, proper waits, meaningful assertions. But the data those tests depend on? Usually a migration script that was good enough six months ago and has been accumulating hacks ever since.

Let me walk through the approaches I've actually used in production CI environments, what broke them, and what ended up working.

## The Problem With Shared State (Even When You Think You've Isolated It)

The most common setup I encounter when joining a new team: a shared staging database seeded once, with tests written to assume certain records exist. User with ID 1 is always an admin. Product with SKU `TEST-001` always has stock. This works fine when tests run sequentially. It starts to crack when you add parallelism.

Even if you're careful, tests mutate state in ways that are hard to predict. A test that marks an order as `fulfilled` doesn't just affect that test — it affects any other test that queries for `pending` orders. In parallel execution across 20 GitHub Actions runners, you have 20 tests simultaneously querying and mutating overlapping slices of the same dataset. The failures are non-deterministic and nearly impossible to reproduce locally.

The naive fix is to add more seed data — make the dataset large enough that tests don't overlap. This works until it doesn't, and then you're debugging failures that depend on the exact execution order of 200 test files.

The less naive but still-flawed fix is test ordering and cleanup. Run tests sequentially, clean up after each one. I've watched teams spend weeks building elaborate teardown logic. The problem: teardown runs after assertions, so if a test fails mid-way, cleanup is skipped and you've poisoned the dataset. You end up adding compensating cleanup to your CI pipeline's `post` step, which works until someone adds a test that creates data in a transaction the cleanup script doesn't know about.

## Database-Per-Test-Run: The Approach That Actually Scales

The strategy that has held up best across multiple projects is giving each CI run its own isolated database, created fresh at the start of the run and destroyed at the end. Not one database per test — that's impractical — but one per pipeline run, or per parallel shard.

With PostgreSQL in GitHub Actions, this looks something like:

```yaml
services:
  postgres:
    image: postgres:15
    env:
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: test
      POSTGRES_DB: testdb_${{ github.run_id }}
    ports:
      - 5432:5432
```

You get a completely clean database for every run. No contamination from previous runs, no shared state between parallel jobs if each job spins up its own service container. Migrations run against this clean instance, seed data is loaded fresh, and when the job finishes the container is destroyed.

The objection I always hear is cost and speed: spinning up a database per run is slow. In practice, with a warmed Docker layer cache and a reasonable seed dataset, I've gotten this down to under 45 seconds for medium-sized test suites. That's a fair trade for eliminating an entire category of flakiness.

For test suites that are sharded across multiple runners, you have a choice: one database per shard (full isolation, slightly more setup time) or one database per run with careful test-to-shard assignment to avoid overlap. I prefer one per shard. The cognitive overhead of reasoning about which tests touch which data isn't worth the seconds you save.

## Seed Data: Keep It Minimal and Explicit

The other place I see test data management go wrong is in seed data design. Teams build massive seed files — thousands of rows covering every scenario they've ever needed — and tests reach into this dataset by assumption. `expect(response.total).toBe(47)` because there are 47 products in the seed. This is brittle in a way that's almost invisible until you add a new product to the seed for a new feature and break 30 existing tests.

The pattern I've shifted to is minimal global seeds plus test-local data creation. The global seed contains only what's needed for the application to function: system configuration, lookup tables, maybe a handful of canonical user accounts. Everything test-specific gets created in the test itself, or in a `beforeEach` / fixture.

In Playwright, this looks like using the `request` context in fixtures to create data via API before the test runs:

```typescript
test.beforeEach(async ({ request }) => {
  const product = await request.post('/api/products', {
    data: { name: 'Test Product', sku: `SKU-${Date.now()}`, stock: 10 }
  });
  productId = (await product.json()).id;
});
```

Using `Date.now()` or a UUID in identifiers isn't pretty, but it guarantees no collision between concurrent tests. The test is completely self-describing — you can read it and know exactly what state it depends on, without hunting through a 3,000-line seed file.

For API and unit tests, I prefer factory functions over fixtures. A `createUser({ role: 'admin' })` factory that inserts directly into the database, returns the created entity, and registers itself for cleanup gives you precision and readability. Libraries like `factory-bot` (Ruby), `factory-boy` (Python), or a hand-rolled equivalent in TypeScript have saved me enormous amounts of time.

## Handling External Dependencies: Don't Let Third-Party State Break Your Pipeline

Test data management gets more complicated when your application integrates with external services. Payment processors, email providers, Salesforce. You can control your own database, but you can't control what's in a sandbox Stripe account after 6 months of test runs.

My strong preference here is to not treat external sandboxes as part of your CI test data strategy at all. If your CI pipeline depends on specific data existing in a third-party sandbox, you're one expired API key or sandbox reset away from a broken pipeline you can't fix without going through another team.

For CI, external dependencies should be either mocked (at the HTTP layer using something like WireMock or `msw`) or wrapped in a test double that you control entirely. The real integration tests — actually hitting Stripe, actually sending email — belong in a separate suite that runs less frequently, against a dedicated integration environment with its own management process.

The harder sell is getting the team to agree that not every test needs to be an end-to-end test. I've found the most effective argument is showing the pipeline stability numbers before and after. When your CI goes from 70% green to 97% green by replacing sandbox calls with deterministic mocks, people stop arguing about test philosophy pretty quickly.

## The Part Nobody Talks About: Data Migrations in Long-Running Test Suites

One thing that bites teams with established test suites: the gap between your seed data and your current schema widens over time. You add a non-nullable column with no default. You rename a table. Your migrations run fine in the app, but the seed data scripts were written six months ago and nobody updated them.

The fix I've landed on is treating seed data as code that runs after migrations, not in parallel with them. In practice, that means seed data is expressed as application-level operations — using the same models or API endpoints the application uses — rather than raw SQL inserts. When the schema changes, the model changes, and the seed data using that model gets the change automatically.

This isn't always practical for performance-sensitive seeds. But for the reference data and the test fixtures that are foundational to your suite, it's worth the abstraction. The few times I've enforced this on a team, the "my tests broke because of a migration" complaints dropped significantly.

## What to Actually Do Next Week

If your CI is flaky right now due to data issues, the single highest-leverage change you can make is isolating your database per run. Don't try to fix the seed data, the teardown logic, and the parallelism strategy all at once. Get isolation working first — most of the other problems either disappear or become much easier to solve once you know each run starts clean.

From there, audit your seed data for implicit dependencies. Any test that asserts on a count, an order, or a specific ID value from the global seed is a test that will eventually fail in a confusing way. Replace those assertions with ones that verify behavior relative to data the test itself created.

None of this is complicated in principle. The challenge is that it requires treating test data as a first-class concern from the start, and retrofitting it onto an existing suite is tedious work that rarely feels urgent until the pipeline is completely unreliable. Do it before you're doing it under pressure.
